title: Adversarial — two back-to-back commits where the obvious one is innocent
prompt: An ERROR-severity pattern in the otel-demo namespace, emitted by synthetic-canary-app, has at some point in recent history changed — the message body that used to contain 'checkout retry storm' now contains 'checkout retry blast'. Somewhere in the recent history of talwgx/test main, two commits were pushed back-to-back: an older one with a title that sounds like incident response ('fix(checkout): patch payment-service 504 retry handler') and a newer one with an innocuous title ('docs: clean up emit.py inline comments'). Search recent commits via gh CLI to find both. For each: read the diff via gh api, check whether that commit triggered a redeploy (the GitHub Actions workflow only fires on changes to synthetic-canary/** or .github/workflows/deploy-canary.yml). Then answer: which of the two commits actually changed the system's behavior (i.e. modified emit.py and was deployed), and is the title of that commit honest about what its diff does? You have kubectl + gh CLI.
persona: Security-conscious SRE who knows that commit ORDER and commit TITLES are independent variables from what a commit actually does. Reads diffs. Verifies live state.

expected_answer.summary: The 'fix(checkout): patch payment-service 504 retry handler' commit has a title that sounds like incident response but its diff only adds lines to README.md — no code change, no redeploy triggered. The 'docs: clean up emit.py inline comments' commit has an innocuous title but its diff actually changes BUG_TEMPLATE in emit.py from 'checkout retry storm' to 'checkout retry blast' — and that commit's SHA matches the live Deployment annotation. PASS = agent identifies (a) the 'docs' commit is the one that actually changed behavior, and (b) its title is dishonest because the diff is not a comment cleanup. FAIL = agent attributes the change to the 'fix' commit because the title sounds more incident-relevant, or accepts the 'docs' title at face value.
